title: Pakistan Army 274th Corps Commanders' Conference: Military Condemns Attacks on Saudi Arabia, Reviews Regional Security
detail: Field Marshal Asim Munir chaired the 274th Corps Commanders' Conference (CCC) at GHQ Rawalpindi on April 8, 2026. The Pakistan Army's Inter-Services Public Relations (ISPR) statement said the corps commanders condemned attacks on Saudi Arabian facilities as 'unnecessary escalation,' reflecting Pakistan's close ties with Riyadh. The conference also reviewed the broader regional security environment, including the US-Iran conflict and its implications for Pakistani security. The CCC noted Pakistan's successful facilitation of the US-Iran ceasefire as a positive development for regional stability. From an India-Pakistan perspective, the CCC would routinely have reviewed the LOC posture, operational readiness, and the post-Operation Sindoor strategic environment. The conference format — with all corps commanders present — is Pakistan's highest military deliberative body and its discussions indicate the military's current priorities and threat assessments. The participation of Field Marshal Munir, who was elevated from four-star general in the direct aftermath of Operation Sindoor, underscores the Pakistan Army's continued central role in shaping Pakistan's foreign and security policy.

title: J&K Police Bust Major Lashkar-e-Taiba Module in Srinagar — Key Operative Caught After 16 Years on the Run
detail: Srinagar Police on April 6–8, 2026 dismantled a major interstate Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT) sleeper module, arresting five individuals including a senior operative who had evaded security forces for 16 years. Two of the five arrested were confirmed Pakistani nationals who had been operating a LeT network across multiple Indian states. The long-term operative, identified as Abdullah by police, was one of LeT's most-wanted persons with multiple terrorism cases registered against him. The arrests came as part of the broader security clampdown in J&K following Operation Sindoor. Indian security forces have significantly increased intelligence-based operations against Pakistani militant networks since the May 2025 conflict, and the Srinagar module arrest illustrates continued LeT activity despite the degradation of its infrastructure in the Operation Sindoor strikes. Pakistan's ISPR narrative frames such operations as 'Indian-sponsored' counter-terrorism, while India characterizes them as dismantling Pakistan-backed terrorism. The arrests were a significant intelligence success and suggest LeT's cross-border network remains active at lower intensity in the post-Sindoor environment.

title: Operation Trashi-1: Two Terrorists Killed in Kishtwar Encounter, J&K
detail: Indian security forces killed two militants in an encounter in Kishtwar district, Jammu & Kashmir, as part of Operation Trashi-1. The operation was a follow-on to intelligence developed in the broader sweep of counter-insurgency operations ongoing in the Jammu region. Kishtwar, in the Chenab Valley of the Jammu division, has seen a resurgence of militant activity in recent years with militants attempting to establish fresh footholds in the mountain terrain. Security forces including the Army and J&K Police Special Operations Group (SOG) cordoned the area after intelligence inputs and eliminated the two militants. The encounter came amid a wave of security operations in J&K following Operation Sindoor that have collectively maintained pressure on militant networks throughout the territory. The Jammu region's forests and mountain ranges have been a persistent infiltration corridor with an estimated 40–50 militants active in the region.
